text: The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space during a laparoscopic polymyomectomy, exposing the ureter and internal iliac artery. The pararectal and pararesical fossae are opened to reveal the medial branch of the internal iliac artery, identified as the left uterine artery. Surrounding tissue is removed for safe proximal control of the uterine artery, which is tracked and prepared for clipping. The uterine artery is clearly visible, and a clip is placed safely, with the assistant tracting medially to expose the left ureter.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the right infundibulopelvic ligament and the round ligament, accessing the avascular retroperitoneal space. The ureter is visible medially, and the internal iliac artery laterally. The pararectal and paravesical spaces are developed, revealing two uterine arteries, which are dissected for improved vascular control.
The surgeon identifies the second uterine artery and places two clips: one on the downward artery and another on the cranial artery, ensuring vascular control for a bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a longitudinal incision between two visible posterior myomas on the uterus. The uterine serosa is tracked laterally by the assistants and surgeon, revealing the myoma capsule as a landmark. Upon reaching the capsule, the procedure progresses with traction and contra-traction to find the correct dissecting plane, cutting vascular attachments surrounding the myoma for a bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a bloodless myomectomy by cutting vascular bridges in coagulating mode, allowing rapid removal of the second myoma. Both myomas are placed in the Douglas sponge. The surgeon takes a large bite of uterine serosa to achieve hemostasis with rapid stitching.

The surgeon uses a 14mm Vicryl needle to perform an intracorporeal knotting technique, carefully invaginating healthy tissue into the myoma bed. The interrupted sutures are placed far from the myoma edges, incorporating large bites of healthy tissue to ensure a strong closure and reduce the risk of postoperative hematoma.

The surgeon demonstrates intracorporeal knotting with a double flat knot technique, ensuring the endometrium remains unopened to minimize postoperative synesthesia risk. The anatomical reconstruction appears well-executed, maintaining a bloodless surgical field.

The surgeon continues the myomectomy procedure, ensuring stitches are placed away from the proximal part of the core tubes in the corneal area. A double flat knot technique is used for invagination, demonstrating effective anatomical reconstruction. The final knot is placed in the upper part of the hystereotomy.

The surgeon ties the final knot, achieving invagination of the tissue with visible blanching at the edges, indicating well-tied knots. The hystereotomy is closed, and the procedure progresses to morcellation using an endobag.

The surgeon performs morcellation using a 50mm diameter morcellator set at 700 RPM within the underbag to minimize the risk of postoperative leiomyomatosis and leiomyosarcoma. The underbag is removed, and the operative field is inspected. The 15mm incision site for the morcellator is closed to reduce the risk of incisional complications. The final view shows the completed laparoscopic myomectomy.
